content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 21, 2017

Eric S. Vail
Hemet City Attorney
1600 Iowa Avenue, Suite 250
Riverside, CA 92507-7426

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-17-162

Dear Mr. Vail:

       This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vince Overmyer, Vice Chair of
the Hemet Planning Commission, regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

       Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

       Does Commissioner Overmyer, based on his employment with Parex USA, Inc., have a
conflict of interest in the following:

       1. Decisions on development projects where stucco or other products manufactured by Parex
USA will be used, but no particular stucco product or manufacturer has been identified as a supplier
for the project?

       2. Development projects where it is unknown if stucco or other products that could be
supplied by Parex USA will be used?

       3. Decisions, such as zone changes and general plan amendments, that do not directly
involve development projects but could spur development that affects the demand for stucco?

       4. Decisions on development projects where Parex USA is identified as a supplier of stucco
or other product that will be used in a development project?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-162
Page No. 2

5. Do any of the answers above change based on the size of the development project or the amount of stucco or other exterior products that are anticipated to be used?

CONCLUSIONS

1 - 4. Except where a Nexus exists (discussed below), the Commissioner will not have a conflict of interest in these decisions. Since they will not be a named party or the subject of a planning commission decision, a decision will not have a foreseeable material financial effect on Parex USA considering its size and prominence in the stucco marketplace.

5. No, the answers above are not based on the size of the project or the amount of the materials needed. With respect to conclusions 1-4, based on the size of Parex USA and sales nationally and internationally, it is unlikely any single project in the city will materially financially affect Parex.

FACTS

Commissioner Overmyer was appointed to the Helmet Planning Commission in February of 2011. The Commissioner is employed by Parex USA, which manufactures exterior insulation and finishes for the construction industry, including stucco and other architectural coatings. Your office clarified on July 13, 2017, that Parex USA's business is to sell its products through retailers and distributors. It does not perform any construction services. On July 13, 2017, your office also clarified that Commissioner Overmyer's position with Parex USA is "Residential Sales Manager." He is responsible for directing regional sales staff to increase sales by calling on plasterers, general contractors, and building material dealers. The Commissioner does not have an ownership interest in Parex USA, his only interest in the company is as a source of income.

You also stated:

• Parex USA is an international business that generates $1.2 billion per year in gross revenues. Within Southern California, Parex USA generates approximately $40 million in gross revenues from sales of stucco and related products each year. Parex USA is one of the two major suppliers of stucco in the Southern California market, and Parex USA currently holds approximately 30 percent market share.

• The exterior stucco that Parex USA provides is the last of a typical three coat process. This exterior is commonly referred to as the color coat.

• A 90-pound bag of stucco covers approximately 10 square yards or 90 square feet. The revenue from each bag of stucco color coat finish is approximately $14. Use on a typical tract home requires about 35 bags of stucco color coat finish depending on the size of the house and other variables. Altogether, this would result in approximately $490 revenue generated per house.

The Planning Commission reviews and approves new development in the City of Hemet. When reviewing individual projects, it is common for presentations to the Commission to include

File No. A-17-162
Page No. 3

product boards and renderings of the project's exterior that specify the materials that are proposed
in the development, including stucco and exterior finish products. From time to time, these
renderings and product boards may identify Parex USA products or the products of its competitors,
or may more generally identify that stucco and other products will be used without identifying
whether a specific manufacturer of that product has been chosen to supply the product.²

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103.
Those interests implicated by your account of the facts are the following:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly,
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033).

• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) including any
community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share of the income of
any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly,
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest (Section 82030(a)).

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

The Commissioner is employed by Parex USA and the company is as a source of income to the
Commissioner. (Section 87103(c) and (d).)

Foreseeability

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest. Parex USA would not be "a named party or

---
² On July 13, 2017, your office clarified that a developer will sometimes bring a Materials Board to present to
the Planning Commission that shows the products they intend to use in construction of their development, such as paint
chips, stucco samples, siding and roofing tiles. Sometimes, a Materials Board will identify the make and model of the
items shown on the Board (e.g. Behr paint in "Eggshell White"). Where a Materials Board is not provided, sometimes a
developer will include the same type of information in high quality color print outs of elevations of their proposed
development.

File No. A-17-162
Page No. 4

the subject of the decision" where a developer merely reveals that their products will be used in
construction of a new development.

If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably
foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable.
(Regulation 18701(b).)

Materiality

Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.)

Nexus. For example, Regulation 18702.3(c) sets forth the nexus test, an alternative
materiality standard potentially applicable to an official's interest in a source of income. The
rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a purpose that
may be advanced by what he or she does as an official, we presume that the private employer is
benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (Torres Advice Letter,
No. 17-117.)

"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source
of income to a public official is deemed material if the public official receives or
is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved,
defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision." (Regulation 18702.3(c).

Typically, a "nexus" is found in situations where the official is also a high-level employee
with direct influence and control over his or her employer's management or policy decisions. It
would not appear that as a sales manager, the Commissioner would be considered a high-level
employee with influence and control over Parex USA's management or policy decisions

The applicability of the nexus test is always premised on the fact that the decision will have
a financial effect on the source. (Todorov Advice Letter, supra.) This is generally a factual
determination. For example, Parex USA could be foreseeably financially affected if, at the time of
the decision, the Commissioner: (1) was soliciting the business on behalf of his employer. In those
cases, a nexus would exist.

Materiality - Source of Income not Explicitly Involved. In all other cases, Regulation
18702.3 provides that since Parex USA is a business entity the materiality standards in Regulation
18702.1. As applicable to your facts, Regulation 18702.1(b) provides:

"[I]f a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a
change in the price of the publicly traded stock, or the value of
a privately held business entity."

Examples set forth in the regulation include decisions to:

File No. A-17-162
Page No. 5

• Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which the business entity is engaged;

• Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is engaged;

• Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies;

• Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives;

• Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the project; or

• Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the business entity

None of these apply to your facts.

More importantly, you note that the decisions of the Planning Commission do not require that a Parex USA product be used. And where Parex USA products are used, you estimated a benefit to Parex USA of approximately $490 per house. Since Parex USA is an international business that generates $1.2 billion per year in gross revenues $40 million in gross revenues within Southern California, any impact on Parex USA by a development project in Hemet would not materially affect it.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

conflict of interest in the following: 
I. Decisions on development projects where stucco or other products manufactured by Parex 
USA will be used, but no particular stucco product or manufacturer has been identified as a supplier

2. Development projects where it is unknown if stucco or other products that could be 
supplied by Parex USA will be used?

involve development projects but could spur development that affects the demand for stucco? 
4. Decisions on development projects where Parex USA is identified as a supplier of stucco 
or other ¹roduct that will be used in a development project? 
1 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in5. Do any of the answers above change based on the size of the development project or the 
amount of stucco or other exterior products that are anticipated to be used?

CONCLUSION: 1 - 4. Except where a Nexus exists (discussed below), the Commissioner will not have a 
conflict of interest in these decisions. Since they will not be a named party or the subject of a

materials needed. With respect to conclusions 1-4, based on the size of Parex USA and sales 
nationally and internationally, it is unlikely any single project in the city will materially financially 
affect Parex.
